---
title: "Cities as Inverted Watersheds"
author: 
- Sanghoon Lee - UBC Sauder
- Seung Hoon Lee - Yonsei Economics
#date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%d %B, %Y')`"
params:
  cell_size: FALSE
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [default, slides.css]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
    seal: false
    mathjax: "https://cdn.bootcss.com/mathjax/2.7.1/MathJax.js?config=TeX-MML-AM_HTMLorMML"
knit: (function(inputFile, encoding) { 
      rmarkdown::render(inputFile,
                    encoding=encoding, 
                    output_file="unist_2025_nov.html") })
---

class: middle, center, .no_slide_number

##.pink_font[Cities as Inverted Watersheds]


<br>

Sanghoon Lee - UBC Sauder

Seung Hoon Lee - Yonsei Economics

<br>

UNIST 

Nov 7, 2025

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, comment=NA)
options(tibble.print_max = Inf)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(htmltools)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tmap)
tmap_mode("view")
library(scales)
library(gt)
```

### Administrative or statistical geographic units

Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs), municipalities, counties, ...

--

Not designed for economic analyses, so often not ideal.
- Los Angeles–Long Beach–Anaheim MSA includes Mojave Desert.

Not available for certain types of analyses
- Subcenters within cities

Different countries use different definitions of cities. 
- Hard to answer questions related to cities (e.g. urbanization and fertility)?

---

### Delineating clusters from granular data

- City boundaries - Rozenfeld et al. (2011), Duranton (2015) 

- Subcenters - McMillen (2001), Redfearn (2007)

- Ethnic enclaves - Dai and Schiff (2023)

- JUE special issue (2021) - de Bellefon et al, Dingle et al, Ch et al, Arribas-Bel et al, Baragwanath et al, Bosker et al, Moreno-Monroy et al, Galdo et al, Henderson et al, Dijkstra et al.

--

Challenges still remain (Duranton, 2021).

- Lack of theoretical foundation

- Arbitrary threshold

- Separate literatures on subcenter and city delineation


---

### This paper

Proposes a new method to delineate clusters, that overcomes these challenges, by applying the .pink_font[watershed segmentation algorithm] used in image processing.

- Considers a city as a mountain on a smoothed density surface of economic activities.

- Applies a technique widely used in medical imaging (organ segmentation in CT scans) to segment cities from spatial data. 

Using the newly proposed method, we delineate for the first time in the literature:

- Subcentres for the entire continental US ([link](https://subcenters.sanghoon.ca/))

- All cities from 1975 to 2020 in the world ([link](https://watersheds.sanghoon.ca/))

Presents new facts from the newly constructed datasets. 


---

class: middle, center

# .pink_font[Method overview]

---

### Flow-based approach

Group contiguous areas that have high level of interactions measured by commuting flows.

- CBSAs in the U.S. 

- FUAs in OECD countries

- Metropolitan Areas in Colombia (Duranton, 2015)

#### Challenge

- Flow data are relatively difficult to obtain (e.g., India, historical data).

---

### Density based approaches

Prevaling approach, due to data availability. 

Includes
- grid-based approach
- DBSCAN
- city clustering algorithm

Set a density threshold and find contiguous areas above the threshold.

--

Main challenge - arbitrary selection of the density threshold.

---

Simple example to illustrate the main challenge in density threshold selection.

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example.png")
```

---

Not possible to separately identify all three clusters with a single threshold.

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_hor.png")
```

---

This can happen both within a city and across cities.

Need to choose the threshold parameters locally.

- Different thresholds across areas make it difficult to compare the identified clusters.
- Arbitrariness in cluster size and characteristics.

Different approaches for delineating subcenters and cities.  

---

### Cities as mountains on a smoothed density surface

We consider cities as *mountains on a smoothed density surface* and the boundaries of cities as the boundaries of mountains. 

We use the watershed segmentation algorithm to identify the boundaries of mountains (cities). 


---

Watershed - the area of land that flows to a common outlet (i.e., a .pink_font[pour point]) and forms a basin.

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="45%", fig.align='center'}
#include_graphics("https://streamline.imgix.net/d0137d76-8f43-408c-9b43-aa3e54f62403/dd342d5f-7adf-4256-9935-7d4b22d7db23/Watershed%20diagram.jpg?ixlib=rb-1.1.0&w=2000&h=2000&fit=max&or=0&s=c559560279d62ca63db72008d5950fdc")
include_graphics("figures/Watershed_diagram.jpeg")
```
.tiny[Source: https://www.rcrcd.org/what-is-a-watershed]

Efficient algorithms and tools available to identify watersheds from GIS raster data.

---

Mountains become watershed basins when inverted!

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_watershed.png")
```

---

The algorithm uniquely classifies points into clusters, given a density surface. No parameters to adjust.

--

The only parameter throughout the whole process is the smoothing parameter to create the density surface.

Setting different values allows us to see the clusters at different detail levels: whole cities with a larger value or subcenters with a smaller value.

---

class: middle

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_diff_sigma.png")
```

---

#### What does our method capture?


--

Imagine a variation of the Monocentric city model with three city centers A, B, and C. 

The commuting flow direction coincides with the direction in which density increases.

The commuting-flow-based approach produces the same delineation as ours.

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width='80%'}
include_graphics("figures/example.png")
```

---

#### Summary

- Use the same algorithm to identify clusters at different levels (e.g. entire cities or subcenters within cities) with a single parameter adjustment. 

- Despite its simplicity, our algorithm outperforms or matches existing methods.

- Theoretical justification and produces similar results as the flow-based approach, without requiring commuting-flow data.

- No need to handpick parameters for each region, allowing large-scale applications.

- Applicable to various data types (e.g., point, polygon, raster), as long as we can generate a density surface.

---

#### Road map

Delineating Consumption Subcenters for continental US ([link](https://subcenters.sanghoon.ca/))

- First time in the literature

Delineating Whole Cities in the US

- Compare methods using the official CBSA boundaries as the benchmark

Delineation of all cities in the world from 1975 to 2020 ([link](https://watersheds.sanghoon.ca/))

--

**Using the new data, we can**
- compare different level of cities (subcenters vs cities).

- compare cities across countries. 

- examine complete city sample, including all cities including very small ones 

- analyze global city dynamics from 1975 to 2020.

---

### New facts from the new data


Hierarchy in business location patterns 
- Clothing Stores (NAICS 4481) and Restaurants (7225) tend to be located in large subcenters and large cities.
- Death Care Services (8122) & Automobile Dealers (4411, 4412) tend to be located in small subceters and small cities. 
- A missing channel in Central Place Theory - land requirement
    
Urban Wage Premium across subcenters vs cities
- Negative wage premium for subcenters within cities
- Negative wage premium for small cities (with pop<10,000)    

City size distribution over time (global)

- Global population growth was driven primarily by the expansion of existing cities, rather than the creation of new ones.


---

### New facts from the new data (continued)

Test important theories with complete world city sample

- Gibrat’s Law: City growth rates are independent of initial size — holds except for the lower tail of the distribution.

- Zipf's Law: Predicts a Pareto distribution of city sizes — actual distribution is closer to lognormal. 

Urbanization patterns from 1975 to 2020

- Median person lived in a city with population 0.66M in 1975, which increased to 1.42M by 2020 (115% increase)

- 90th quantile person lived in a city with population 4.3M in 1975, which increased to 11.7M by 2020 (172% incresae)



---

class: middle, center

# .pink_font[Delineating Consumption Subcenters]

---

### Safegraph data

U.S.
- POIs (Points of Interest) for the whole continental US
- Visited in 2019
- 4.6 million places with lon and lat.

Exclude the POIs with the NAICS codes starting with 11 (Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting), 21 (Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction), 22 (Utilities), 31-33 (Manufacturing), 55 (Management of Companies and Enterprises).

---

.scroll-output[
.small[
```{r echo=FALSE}
# temp <- read_rds("../data/pois_visited_2019.rds") %>% filter(!str_extract(naics_code, "^\\d{2}") %in% c("11", "21", "22", "31", "32", "33", "55"))
# atlanta_boundry <- st_read("../data/cont_us/cbsa_boundaries.shp") %>% filter(NAME %>% str_detect("Atlanta"))
# temp %>% st_intersection(atlanta_boundry) %>% write_rds("pois_presentation.rds")
pois_sfm <- read_rds("tables/pois_presentation.rds")

set.seed(123)
sgraph_sample <- pois_sfm %>% select(`POI Name`=location_name, `NAICS`=naics_code) %>% sample_n(100) %>%
  st_transform(crs=4326) %>% 
  mutate(Longitude=st_coordinates(.)[,1], Latitude=st_coordinates(.)[,2] ) %>% 
  st_drop_geometry() 

#xtable::xtable(sgraph_sample %>% head(15), 
#               caption="SafeGraph Data Example",
#               label="tab:safe") %>% 
#  print(file="../doc/draft_v1/safe_graph_example.tex")

sgraph_sample %>%   knitr::kable()
```
]]

---

```{r echo=F}
include_graphics("figures/pois_atlanta.png")
```

---

class: middle, center
.pink_font[
## Processing steps
]

---

### Convert POI data to a raster data.

Project the POI data to .pink_font[USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic, USGS (EPSG = 5070)].
- Preserve area size.
- Use meters as distance unit.

Create a raster data with a 100 meter by 100 meter resolution, covering the whole continental US.

Count the number of POIs for each cell.

---

class: middle

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="tm_pois_atl.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> POIs (Atlanta downtown)</figcaption>
  </figure>
  
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="tm_raster_atl.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Raster</figcaption>
  </figure>
</div>

---

### Smoothing the Surface

.pull-left[

We apply the Gaussian filter to the raster data.

.pink_font[Two dimensional moving average] with the Gaussian kernel as weights.

$$g(x,y) = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sigma^2} \exp^{-(x^2+y^2)/(2\sigma^2)}$$

x and y are the distances from the origin in the horizontal and the vertical axes.
]

.pull-right[
```{r echo=FALSE, out.height="30%", fig.align='center'}
#include_graphics("https://i0.wp.com/theailearner.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/normal4.png?w=721&ssl=1")
#.tiny[Source: https://theailearner.com/2019/05/06/gaussian-blurring/]
include_graphics("figures/gaussian_figure.png")
```

]

With $\sigma=500m$: cells 500m, 1000m, and 1500m away get 61%, 14%, and 1% weights compared to the own cell. With $\sigma=1km$:  88%, 61%, and 32% weights.

---

class: middle

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="tm_raster_atl_large_nobox.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Raster </figcaption>
  </figure>
  
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="tm_raster_atl_gaussian.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> After Gaussian smoothing </figcaption>
  </figure>
</div>

---

### Invert the smoothed surface

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="tm_raster_atl_gaussian.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Pre-inverted surface </figcaption>
  </figure>
  
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="inv_atl.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Inverted surface </figcaption>
  </figure>
</div>

---

### Create D8 flow pointer

Shows flow direction for each cell.

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.align='center'}
#include_graphics("https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/GUID-A541AFD3-922C-41DA-952C-0D4524F85B39-web.gif")

include_graphics("figures/d8_flow_algorithm.png")
```

<!-- Pour Points = local minima of the inverted smoothed surface, but not local maxima. -->
<!-- - The D8 flow pointer of the .pink_font[inverted] surface is 0. -->
<!-- - The D8 flow pointer of the .pink_font[pre-inverted] surface is .pink_font[not] 0, to exclude flat areas. -->

---

### Identifying peaks

Pour points of the inverted smoothed surface.

Local minima of the inverted smoothed surface, but not local maxima.
- The D8 flow pointer of the .pink_font[inverted] surface is 0.
- The D8 flow pointer of the .pink_font[pre-inverted] surface is .pink_font[not] 0, to exclude flat areas.

---

class: middle

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">
  
  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="inv_atl.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Inverted surface </figcaption>
  </figure>
  
    <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="inv_atl_peak.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Pour points (peaks) </figcaption>
  </figure>

</div>

---

### Identify watersheds

For each pour point, identify the watershed cells flowing to the pour point using the D8 flow pointer.

---

class: middle

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">

    <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="inv_atl_peak.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Pour points (peaks) </figcaption>
  </figure>

  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="watersheds_atl.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Watersheds </figcaption>
  </figure>

</div>

---

### Count the number of POIs for each watershed.

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">

  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="watersheds_atl.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Watersheds </figcaption>
  </figure>

    <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="watersheds_pois_atl.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
    <figcaption> Watersheds with N POIs </figcaption>
  </figure>

</div>

---

#### Final output with $\sigma$ = 500 meters

<!-- #generated by "density_based_benchmark/density_based_benchmark_r100_s5.R" -->
<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">

  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="atl_ours_5.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
  </figure>

    <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="ny_ours_5.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
  </figure>

</div>

---

#### Final output with $\sigma$ = 800 meters

<!-- #generated by "density_based_benchmark/density_based_benchmark_r100_s5.R" -->
<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">

  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="atl_ours_8.png" alt="Image 2" style="width: 100%;">
  </figure>

  <figure style="text-align: center;">
    <img src="ny_ours_8.png" alt="Image 1" style="width: 100%;">
  </figure>

</div>

---


class: middle, center

# .pink_font[Delineating Whole Cities in the US]

---

Different $\sigma$'s reveal clusters at different levels.

With a large smoothing parameter, we can delineate US cities. ([link](https://subcenters.sanghoon.ca/))

--

Using the official CBSA boundaries as a benchmark, we can compare our algorithm against the density-based method.

--

We use grids with 1 km x 1 km cell resolution to reduce computation costs.

---

### Choosing $\sigma$

- We select $\sigma$ to ensure our delineated boundaries closely match the CBSA boundaries.

- To quantify the similarity, we employ the City Jaccard (similarity) index $J_C$ (Bellefon et al, 2021).

$$J_C \equiv \frac{\sum_{k \in K} | U_k^{cbsa} \cap U_k^{ws}|}{|  U^{cbsa} \cup U^{ws} |}$$
    where $U_k^{cbsa}$ refers to CBSA $k$ and $U_k^{ws}$ denotes the cluster corresponding to CBSA $k$.

- We can measure the size of a set $|\cdot|$ using population or area size.

---

### Optimal $\sigma = 12km$

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='80%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/jaccard.png")
```


---

### Our Delineated Cities

[SD = 12 (12 cells x 1) kilometers](maps/cont_us_c1000_s12_ge100_pois_dcolor.html)

With $\sigma = 12km$, the Jaccard indices are 0.850 with population and 0.482 with area.

We identify a total of 2,189 cities.

The smallest one contains only 2 points of interest (POIs), the median contains 265 POIs, and the largest city encompasses 275,677 POIs.

---

Watershed cities vs. POI density

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/usa_density_watershed_map.png")
```
]

---

CBSAs vs. POI density

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/usa_density_map.png")
```
]

---

### Optimal $\sigma$ and the density threshold

We tune parameters of the density-based approach to best match the CBSA boundaries.  

- Jaccard index with Areas: $\sigma$ = 6. threshold=0.4. Jaccard index = 0.137

 Ours: $\sigma$ = 12. Jaccard index = 0.482.

- Jaccard index with Population: $\sigma$ = 3. threshold=1.3. Jaccard index = 0.683

 Ours: $\sigma$ = 12. Jaccard index = 0.850.

--

#### Compared to the density based method

Our method produces a superior result with only one parameter.

The optimal parameter value is same both with area and population.

---

Optimal density-based clusters with areas ($\sigma=6$ and threshold=0.4 )

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/us_map_den_sig_60_cut_0.004.png")
```
]


---

Optimal density-based clusters with population ($\sigma =3$ and threshold=1.3 )

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/us_map_den_sig_27_cut_0.012.png")
```
]


---

A single threshold tends to over-represent large clusters and under-represent small ones.

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_hor2.png")
```




---

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
#generated by "tasks/calc_jaccard_index/jaccard_index_density_based4_dev_analysis.R"
include_graphics("figures/scatter_ws_density.png")
```
]

---

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
#generated by "tasks/calc_jaccard_index/jaccard_index_density_based4_dev_analysis.R"
include_graphics("figures/scatter_ws_density_zoom.png")
```
]

---

### New Fact - Hierarchy in business location patterns

What business types tend to concentrate in larger subcenters (vs larger cities)?  

According to Central Place Theory, higher-order services (e.g. general hospital, museum) require larger markets due to thin demand or high fixed cost and thus tend to be concentrated in larger cities. 

Verifying this pattern at the subcenter level or in small cities has been difficult, due to the lack of consistent boundary data.

A potential missing channel in Central Place Theory: higher-order services may also require larger land areas.

We compute the average cluster sizes for 4-digit NAICS codes at both the subcenter and city levels.


---

#####  POI Types (NAICS) by Avg. Cluster Size (among 4D-NAICS codes with more than 20,000 POIs)

```{r echo=FALSE}
naics_hierarchy <- read_rds("tables/avg_npois_combined.rds") %>% filter(unit=="Consumption Cluster")
top15 <- naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>% 
  head(15) %>% 
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Top 15`=desc, `NAICS`=naics_code, `Cluster Size (Avg. N of POIs)`=avg_n_pois) %>% 
  knitr::kable()

bottom15 <- naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(avg_n_pois) %>% 
  head(15) %>%   arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>%
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Bottom 15`=desc,  `NAICS`=naics_code, `Cluster Size (Avg. N of POIs)`=avg_n_pois) %>%   
  knitr::kable()
```

.pull-left[
.tiny[
```{r echo=FALSE}
top15
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.tiny[
```{r echo=FALSE}
bottom15
```
]]


---

#####  POI Types (NAICS) by Avg. Cluster Size at City Level (among 4D-NAICS codes with more than 20,000 POIs)

```{r echo=FALSE}

naics_hierarchy <- read_rds("tables/avg_npois_combined.rds") %>% filter(unit=="City")

```

.pull-left[
.tiny[
```{r echo=FALSE}
naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>% 
  head(15) %>% 
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Top 15`=desc, `NAICS`=naics_code, `Cluster Size (Avg. N of POIs)`=avg_n_pois) %>% 
  knitr::kable()
```
]
]

.pull-right[
.tiny[
```{r echo=FALSE}
naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(avg_n_pois) %>% 
  head(15) %>% arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>%
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Bottom 15`=desc,  `NAICS`=naics_code, `Cluster Size (Avg. N of POIs)`=avg_n_pois) %>%   
  knitr::kable()
```
]]


---

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width='60%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/avg_npois_combined.png")
```

---

### New Fact - Urban Wage Premium for subcenters and cities

Urban Wage Premium: Average wages tend to rise with city size.
	
- This relationship is well-documented and holds across many countries.

Natural questions to answer with the new data:

- Does the Urban Wage Premium hold among subcenters within the same city?
	
- Does it persist in smaller cities?

By applying areal interpolation to Census data, we estimate average income and population for each watershed cluster.

-	Areal interpolation is a spatial estimation method used to transfer data from one set of geographic boundaries (e.g., census tracts) to another (e.g., custom-defined clusters).

- This allows us to estimate demographic and economic characteristics for non-standard spatial units such as our watershed clusters.

---

#### Urban Wage Premium for subcenters

- Subcenter-level regression

$$log(Income_{i,j}) = \beta_1 \times log(Population_i) + \delta_j + \epsilon_{i,j}$$
where $Income_i$ refers to the average income of subcenter $i$ in city $j$


- City-level regression

$$log(Income_{j}) = \beta_1 \times log(Population_j) + \epsilon_j$$
**Note:** Regressions are weighted by population sizes. 

---

#### Regression outcomes

```{r echo=FALSE}
# 데이터 준비
tab <- tribble(
  ~term,                ~`Sub-center`, ~City,
  "log(pop)",           "-0.0649 ***", "0.0791 ***",
  "",                   "(0.0067)",    "(0.0019)",
  "(Intercept)",        "",            "9.2651 ***",
  "",                   "",            "(0.0269)",
  "N",                  "104,749",     "2,190",
  "R²",                 "0.2729",      "0.4433",
  "N of group: city",   "2,083",       ""
)

# gt로 표 생성
tab %>%
  gt(rowname_col = "term") %>%
  cols_label(
    `Sub-center` = "Sub-center",
    City = "City"
  ) %>%
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = everything()   # 모든 열 가운데 정렬
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**p < 0.01; *p < 0.05; · p < 0.1**")
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = px(16),   # 폰트 크기 키우기
    table.width = pct(100),     # 표 너비 페이지 꽉 차게
    heading.title.font.size = px(18),
    data_row.padding = px(8)    # 셀 간격 넓히기
  )
```


---

#### Urban Wage Premium for small cities (Benchmark)

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='40%'}
include_graphics("figures/urban_premium_official_ws.png")
```

**Official CBSA data:** 0.0789 (s.e. 0.0031)

**Watersheds with pop > 10,000:** 0.0625 (s.e. 0.0043)

---

#### Urban Wage Premium for small cities (all cities)

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='35%'}
include_graphics("figures/urban_premium_offical_ws_all_sample.png")
```

Urban Wage Premium may not work for small cities. 

**Watersheds with pop > 10,000:** 0.0625 (s.e. 0.0043)

**Watersheds with pop < 10,000:** -0.0270 (s.e. 0.0007)


---

class: middle, center

# .pink_font[Delineating all cities in the world]

### .pink_font[1975 to 2020]

---

### GHS population data

Using the Global Human Settlement (GHS) population data, we delineate cities in the world. 

- High resolution (250m x 250m) population grids derived from satellite imagery and census data developed by the European Commission's Joint Research Centre.

- Data spans from 1975 to 2020 in 5-year increments.


---

### Delineation of all cities in the world from 1975 to 2020.

Using the official CBSA boundaries as a benchmark, we choose $\sigma= 9 km$ that our delineated boundaries closely match the CBSA boundaries, as measured by the City Jaccard index. 

Then, we apply the tuned $\sigma$ globally and delineate cities in the world from 1975 to 2020.

Using the outcomes ([Link](http://watersheds.sanghoon.ca/)), we can compare cities across countries and over time. 

---

### City Size Distribution Over Time

```{r echo=FALSE}
# 데이터 생성
tab <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Year, ~`N of Clusters`, ~Q1,     ~Median,  ~Q3,     ~Max,
  1975,  35584,            1869,    13543,    70520,   27614044,
  2000,  36628,            2291,    18159,    98046,   34193148,
  2020,  36653,            2561,    20706,    119018,  40100568
)

# gt로 표 생성
tab %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = -Year,
    decimals = 0, 
    use_seps = TRUE
  ) %>%
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = everything()
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = px(14),
    table.width = pct(100)
  )
```

Global population growth has been driven mainly by the expansion of existing cities, rather than by the creation of new cities.


---

### City Size Dynamics - Gibrat's Law for all cities in the World

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='70%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/city_dynamics.png")
```

Eeckhout's findings (2004) support Gibrat’s law for U.S. cities from 1990 to 2020, except for the bottom of the distribution. Our results are broadly consistent. 

---

### Zipf's Law for all Cities in the World

Zipf's Law states that a city's size is inversely proportional to its rank.

$$S_r = \frac{S_1}{r}$$
where $S_r$ is the size of city with rank $r$ and $S_1$ is the size of the largest city. 

--

Three ways to test Zipf's Law:

1. Run Zipf regression and check if Zipf's coefficient $-\beta_1$ is close to 1. 
$$log(Rank_i + 1) =\beta_0 + \beta_1 \times log(Population_i) + \epsilon_i$$
2. Examine if city size distribution follows Pareto distribution with shape parameter 1. 

3. Zipf's coefficient is stable across different minimum population cutoffs of the sample.

---

### Zipf regression outcome

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
#gereated by watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R
include_graphics("figures/zips_world.png")
```
]

---

### Distribution of city sizes in the world (1975 and 2020)

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%', fig.align='center'}
#gereated by watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R
include_graphics("figures/world_city_dist.png")
```

City size distribution is close to log normal distribution, not Pareto distribution. 

---

### Zipf's Coefficient depending on population cutoff

.center2[
```{r, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/zips_world_cut.png")
```
]

---

**City size relative to the largest city (with pop $>$ 50M in 2020)**

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='90%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/zipf_7520.png")
```

<span style="color:red">Red</span> line shows relative sizes predicted by Zipf's Law. 

---

### Rising Urbanization - City Size of the Median Resident

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='95%', fig.align='center'}
#generated by "watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R"
include_graphics("figures/medium_person_city.png")
```

Rank all people by their city’s population; the median person’s city size is the population of the city where that person lives.

---

### Median Resident's City Size (1975 vs 2020)

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='80%', fig.align='center'}
#generated by "watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R"
include_graphics("figures/med_pop_75_20.png")
```



---

class: middle, center

# .pink_font[Conclusion]

---

This paper proposes a new method to delineate clusters by utilizing the watershed segmentation algorithm.

- Our method has theoretical background and produces similar outcomes as the flow-based methods, but does not require flow data.
- We do not have to adjust parameter by location, allowing large scale applications.
- We have only one global parameter $\sigma$. We can use the same algorithm to identify the clusters of different levels, simply adjusting the parameter.
- Our method can be applied to any type of spatial data as long as we can generate a density surface, such as pixel-based data (e.g. night-light satellite images) and polygon-based data (e.g., Census data)

By applying our algorithm to POI data and satelite-based population data, we delineate:
- consumption subcenters in the continental US. First time in the literature.
- cities in the world from 1975 to 2020. 

These new datasets allow us to provide new empirical facts about urban structure and dynamics.
